bins.name: Treat ESR charging distinctly
bins.desc: GridStor's central position is that ESR charging is integral to a supply resource and must be treated separately from large customer load. It warns that applying new large-load requirements to ESRs would slow storage deployment and asks the Commission to consider ESRs distinctly in any subsequent rule.
quotes: GridStor submits these comments to assist the Commission as it moves forward with a rulemaking on these issues, emphasizing the need to distinguish the treatment of Energy Storage Resource (“ESR”) charging in the development of a Final Rule or other action that affects the interconnection of large loads.
Energy Storage Resources (ESRs) are critical to helping get large loads online faster and reliably.
In subsequent action to this ANOPR, the Commission must carefully distinguish ESR charging from large customer loads.
To the extent that the Commission seeks to put new requirements on large loads, it is imperative that ESRs be considered distinctly and separately from large loads.

bins.name: Storage flexibility serves large loads
bins.desc: GridStor argues that batteries are inherently flexible and curtailable, enabling demand management for data centers and helping large loads meet ride-through and curtailment requirements. It wants reforms for flexible loads to recognize and extend to ESRs.
quotes: Energy Storage Resources (ESRs) are critical to helping get large loads online faster and reliably.
Batteries enable flexible demand management for data centers to ensure business continuity while complying with such requirements.

To the extent that the Commission seeks to develop rules to expedite transmission service studies of large loads that are flexible and curtailable, we respectfully request that such rules also enable speedy and timely transmission service studies of ESR charging.
It is also worth exploring whether RTOs/ISOs might offer different forms of transmission service to support such flexible and curtailable loads.

Other large loads and ESRs would benefit from a “conditional firm” transmission service, where pre-defined conditions (e.g., EEA, conservative operations, etc) require curtailment but where transmission service is otherwise expected—an improvement from as-available service that has little predictability.

bins.name: Avoid discriminatory rules against ESRs
bins.desc: GridStor asks that new large-load rules not inadvertently disadvantage ESRs, calling it imperative that ESRs be considered separately and that large-load queuing requirements not be imposed on resources already in generator queues.
quotes: To the extent that the Commission seeks to put new requirements on large loads, it is imperative that ESRs be considered distinctly and separately from large loads.

For example, it would be inappropriate and onerously duplicative to apply large loads queuing requirements as discussed in the ANOPR to apply to ESRs. Today, ESRs already participate in generator interconnection queuing processes with required deposits and readiness requirements, and ESRs execute interconnection agreements in a well-understood manner.

bins.name: No duplicative ESR queue deposits
bins.desc: GridStor opposes applying large-load queuing and deposit requirements to ESRs, noting that ESRs already participate in generator interconnection queues with required deposits and readiness requirements, making added requirements onerously duplicative.
quotes: For example, it would be inappropriate and onerously duplicative to apply large loads queuing requirements as discussed in the ANOPR to apply to ESRs. Today, ESRs already participate in generator interconnection queuing processes with required deposits and readiness requirements, and ESRs execute interconnection agreements in a well-understood manner.

bins.name: Coordinated study of paired ESR and load
bins.desc: GridStor supports the ANOPR proposal to study paired load and generation together but asks that the load side, including ESR charging, be included so paired ESRs and large loads are studied as a single coordinated resource rather than competing for transmission capacity. It recommends ESR charging be studied during the generator interconnection process before the agreement is executed.
quotes: In particular, we ask that any Commission consideration of such coordinated study include the load side of paired facilities like ESRs, not only the generation side.

The present approach to studying loads independently creates a competitive dynamic between any paired ESR and large loads, forcing them to vie for transmission capacity rather than provide desired mutual benefit.
The Commission should enable studies of large loads and paired ESRs as coordinated loads, i.e., with integrated control that act as a single resource.

Therefore, we recommend that the Commission consider whether study of ESR charging should be more optimally conducted during the generator interconnection process and prior to a generator interconnection agreement, instead of in a separate load study after a generator interconnection agreement.

bins.name: Integrated study of paired facilities
bins.desc: GridStor wants paired large loads and ESRs studied as coordinated loads with integrated control, arguing that independent study forces them to vie for transmission capacity and delays and raises the cost of both.
quotes: The present approach to studying loads independently creates a competitive dynamic between any paired ESR and large loads, forcing them to vie for transmission capacity rather than provide desired mutual benefit.
The Commission should enable studies of large loads and paired ESRs as coordinated loads, i.e., with integrated control that act as a single resource.
In particular, we ask that any Commission consideration of such coordinated study include the load side of paired facilities like ESRs, not only the generation side.

bins.name: Recognize co-located load and storage
bins.desc: GridStor frames ESR charging paired with large load like a co-located behind-the-meter configuration and asks the Commission to recognize this pairing when distinguishing ESR treatment and coordinating studies.
quotes: GridStor submits these comments to assist the Commission as it moves forward with a rulemaking on these issues, emphasizing the need to distinguish the treatment of Energy Storage Resource (“ESR”) charging in the development of a Final Rule or other action that affects the interconnection of large loads.
In particular, we ask that any Commission consideration of such coordinated study include the load side of paired facilities like ESRs, not only the generation side.
The Commission should enable studies of large loads and paired ESRs as coordinated loads, i.e., with integrated control that act as a single resource.

bins.name: Expedite curtailable load and ESR studies
bins.desc: GridStor supports the ANOPR proposal to expedite study of curtailable and dispatchable loads and requests that expedited transmission service studies also apply to ESR charging, citing existing 60 to 90 day load interconnection timeframes as a model.
quotes: To the extent that the Commission seeks to develop rules to expedite transmission service studies of large loads that are flexible and curtailable, we respectfully request that such rules also enable speedy and timely transmission service studies of ESR charging.
Precedent exists in RTO/ISO tariffs for load interconnection processes that need no longer than 60-90 days to execute fully, and similar timeframes could be applied to ESR charging studies.

bins.name: New transmission service options
bins.desc: GridStor suggests RTOs/ISOs offer new forms of transmission service for flexible loads and ESRs, including a non-firm service driven by locational marginal pricing and a conditional firm service that curtails only under predefined conditions.
quotes: It is also worth exploring whether RTOs/ISOs might offer different forms of transmission service to support such flexible and curtailable loads.

Other large loads and ESRs would benefit from a “conditional firm” transmission service, where pre-defined conditions (e.g., EEA, conservative operations, etc) require curtailment but where transmission service is otherwise expected—an improvement from as-available service that has little predictability.

bins.name: Fill the Order 841 to 2023 gap
bins.desc: GridStor recommends FERC fill the gap between Order 841, which declined to require study of ESR charging in the generator interconnection process, and Order 2023, which gave directives on how to study it, by allowing ESR charging to be studied during interconnection before the generator interconnection agreement.
quotes: To that end, the Commission should move reforms to fill the gap left between Order 841 and Order 2023.

Therefore, we recommend that the Commission consider whether study of ESR charging should be more optimally conducted during the generator interconnection process and prior to a generator interconnection agreement, instead of in a separate load study after a generator interconnection agreement.

bins.name: Established cost allocation for ESR upgrades
bins.desc: GridStor states that upgrades identified for ESR charging should be allocated under the same established cost-allocation principles applied to other interconnection upgrades.
quotes: Such upgrades would be allocated following the established cost-allocation principles applied to other interconnection upgrades.

bins.name: ESR charging upgrade cost allocation
bins.desc: GridStor addresses how the costs of network upgrades needed for ESR charging should be handled, recommending they follow the established interconnection cost-allocation framework rather than a separate treatment.
quotes: Such upgrades would be allocated following the established cost-allocation principles applied to other interconnection upgrades.
